segments.s760-g17: بخورد is 3rd-person past of خوردن ('he ate/consumed'); here it reports that the poison/food was taken. و links two clauses ('and'). یکی هفته زان پس is a temporal phrase: یکی هفته = 'one week', زان پس = 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after'). بزیست (ب + زیست) is past tense meaning 'he lived' or 'remained alive' — together the clause says he took it and then lived another week.
segments.s760-g18: هرآنکس که introduces a free relative clause 'whoever'. بشنید is past tense 'heard'. بروی here is idiomatic/poetic and not the literal verb 'to go' — it functions as part of the phrase meaning 'openly/at once' or a manner adverb modifying گریست; the exact lexeme is archaic. گریست is past 'wept/lamented'. Together: 'whoever heard (this) wept.'
segments.s760-g19: همی is a poetic particle here giving a sense of immediacy or emphasis ('thus/then'). پادشاهی is the subject 'kingship/kingdom'. به پایان رسید is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'reached its end' (به = toward/into, پایان = end, رسید = reached). The clause states that the kingship was ended.
segments.s760-g20: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = from, هر = every, سو = side/direction). همی again gives immediacy/emphasis. دشمن آمد پدید is a two-verb sequence: آمد 'came' and پدید 'appeared/manifested' — together 'the enemy came into view' or 'the enemy appeared.'
segments.s760-g21: چنین است = 'such is' or 'thus is' (چنین = thus/so, است = is). کردار گردنده دهر is a nominal phrase: کردار 'deed/conduct' qualified by گردنده دهر 'the revolving/turning age' (گردنده modifies دهر). Put together: 'Such is the conduct of the turning world.'
segments.s760-g22: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/observe' (نگه = look/watch, کن = do!). کزو = 'from which / from it' (k-e-zu), چند = 'how much' or 'how many', یابی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive of یافتن 'you may find/you find', تو is the pronoun 'you', بهر = 'for/advantage'. The clause is imperative/instructive: 'Look and see how much you can gain from it.' Note the poetic word order (the object and modifiers follow the verb).
segments.s760-g23: بخور is the imperative 'eat' or idiomatically 'enjoy/consume' (here an exhortation). هرچ = 'whatever/whatever thing', داری = 'you have' (present). به فردا مپای is a negative admonition: به فردا = 'on/for tomorrow', مپای is an archaic negative or prohibitive verbal form meaning 'do not lean/hope/rely (upon)' — the poetical phrasing warns against trusting tomorrow.
segments.s760-g24: که introduces the reason/explanation 'for/because' (or 'that'). فردا = 'tomorrow/the future'. مگر here means 'unless' or 'unless perhaps' (introducing an exception) and دیگر = 'other/another'. آیدش is 'it comes to him/it comes' (آید + enclitic ش), and رای here means 'plan/counsel' — so 'for tomorrow, unless another thing occurs, may bring a different plan' i.e. 'tomorrow may have another plan.'
segments.s760-g25: ستاند is an archaic/poetic perfective verb here meaning 'took' (from ستاندن); ز تو is a prepositional phrase 'from you'; دیگری را is a definite object with را marking the thing/person given; دهد is a present-tense verb 'gives' (here used with the perfective to express a general or proverbial sequence: 'takes from you and gives to another').
segments.s760-g26: جهان خوانیش = جهانِ خوان + ـش: 'the world's banquet/feast' with the possessive suffix -ش ('its/his'); بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'; بر here means 'upon/depends on' and جهد is a noun 'effort/striving' — the clause says the world's feast is, without doubt, contingent on effort.
segments.s760-g27: بخور is the imperative 'eat'; هرچ داری is a relative phrase 'whatever you have' (هرچ = 'whatever', داری = 'you have'); فزونی بده is another imperative 'give the excess/surplus' with فزونی meaning 'excess' and بده the 2nd-person imperative 'give'.
segments.s760-g28: تو رنجیده‌ای is a stative/perfect form 'you are distressed/angry' with explicit subject تو for emphasis; بهر denotes 'for/on account of' and دشمن 'enemy'; منه here is an archaic negative imperative 'do not' (poetic form equivalent to مکن), so the phrase means 'do not be upset for the enemy.'
segments.s760-g29: هرآنگه که is a temporal conjunction 'whenever/when'; روز تو اندر گذشت literally 'your day passed' — گذشت is the past participle/past 'passed' used here to mean 'when your time is over' and اندر is a preposition 'in/within'.
segments.s760-g30: نهاده is a past participle 'placed/laid (aside)' (i.e., 'what you have set down'); همه 'all' is the subject; باد is the noun 'wind' (here metaphorical); گردد is a present/subjunctive-form of 'become' ('becomes' or 'may become'); به دشت 'into the plain' — together: 'all that you have laid down will turn into wind on the plain.'
segments.s760-g1: ز + place names is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from (the town)'; the verb راخواندند is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they summoned/called'. Word order puts the place phrase first (they called him from Jahrom).
segments.s760-g2: بران is an archaic/poetic compound (بر + آن) meaning 'upon that' or 'on'; تخت شاهیش is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the kingly/royal throne' (شاهیش qualifying تخت). بنشاندند is 3rd person plural past 'they seated/installed (him)', with the object (Farrokhzad) understood from the previous clause.
segments.s760-g3: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; برتخت is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'on the throne' (بر + تخت). بنشست is 3rd person singular past 'he sat (down)'. کرد is a light verb 'he made/did' used with آفرین to mean 'he voiced an acclamation'.
segments.s760-g4: ز is causal here 'for/because of'; نیکی دهش is a noun phrase where نیکی 'goodness' and دهش 'the giving/grant' combine to mean 'the giving of goodness'; بر links the gift to the recipient (here 'to' the Creator), and جهان‌آفرین is the compound 'world‑creator' (the Creator).
segments.s760-g5: منم is the quoted utterance 'I am' (or 'it is I'); گفت is the speech verb 'said' (3rd person past) and is followed by the speaker noun; فرزند شاهنشهان is the subject ('the son of the shahanshah') with شاهنشهان as a genitive/possessive. The order is the common inversion where the quoted words appear before the verb 'گفت'.
segments.s760-g6: نخواهم is 1st person negative of 'to want' used here idiomatically to mean 'I desire nothing' or 'I want nothing'; جز ... از = 'except (for)'; از links the exception to ایمنی 'safety/security'; در جهان is an adverbial phrase 'in the world'.
segments.s760-g7: ز in this poetic register marks scope 'in/from the world' (here adverbial), هرآنکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever (that)'; جوید is present/factual 'seeks/wants' (3rd person), and گزند is the object 'harm'.
segments.s760-g8: چو here is conditional 'if'; شاه باشم uses the present/subjunctive 'باشم' ('if I am/become king') with من omitted after چو but understood; نگردد is a negative form of 'to become' (here referring back to 'whoever') and has a future/result sense 'will not become', and بلند is the predicate adjective 'high/exalted'.
segments.s760-g9: هر آنکس که = a relative phrase 'whoever / every person who'. جوید is the present (classical/poetic) form 'seeks' (verb) and takes the following prepositional phrase به دل راستی ('truth in the heart' / 'sincerity of heart') as its object/goal. Word order is classical: relative phrase → verb → prepositional object; راستی is a noun 'truth' qualifying the inner state (دل).
segments.s760-g10: نیارد is here a negative/poetic form meaning 'does not bring' / 'will not cause' (the provided lemma senses do not include this verbal reading, so the precise sense is irregular/poetic). به کار اندرون = 'to/for the inner affairs' (به + کار, اندَرون qualifying 'کار' = 'the inward / inner business'); کاستی = 'deficiency / lack'. The clause says that seeking sincerity in the heart will not result in lack in one's inner affairs.
segments.s760-g11: بدارمش is a verb with enclitic object ـش ('him') — in context it means 'I will hold / treat / keep him' (the provided senses list is misleading—one listed sense is 'hang/execute' but here the verb functions as a transitive verb taking ـش). چون = 'as / like'. جان پاک ارجمند = a nominal phrase 'a pure, precious soul' (جان = 'life/soul', پاک = 'pure', ارجمند = 'precious/esteemed').
segments.s760-g12: نجویم is the negative-present (ن + جویم) meaning 'I do not seek/desire'. ابر بی‌گزندان = 'a cloud devoid of harm' (a poetic way to mean 'the harmless' or 'the blameless'); گزند = 'harm'. The clause literally says 'I do not seek harm for (to) a harmless cloud' — i.e. 'I do not wish to harm the blameless.' Note the verb precedes its object, a common classical word order.
segments.s760-g13: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). یک ماه بگذشت = 'a month passed' (بگذشت is past tense 'passed'). بر تخت اوی = 'upon his throne' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', اوی enclitic = 'his'). Classical sequence: temporal marker + duration + past verb + locative phrase.
segments.s760-g14: بخاک اندر آمد = 'came down to the ground / into the dust' (بخاک = 'to the ground/into dust', اندر = 'into/within', آمد = 'came'). سر و بخت اوی = 'his head and his fortune' (سر = 'head', و = 'and', بخت = 'fortune/luck', اوی = 'his'). The clause pictures his downfall: both his person (head) and his luck fell to the earth.
segments.s760-g15: همین بودش = 'this was his (state)' (همین = 'this', بودش = 'it was / it was to him' with enclitic). از روز و آرام بهر = 'through day and quiet/rest' (از + روز = 'from/by day', و آرام = 'and repose/quiet', بهر = 'for / as to'). The clause sets up his outward calm condition or circumstances.
segments.s760-g16: یکی بنده = 'a certain servant' (یکی = 'a certain', بنده = 'servant'). در می‌برآمیخت زهر = 'was mixing poison in/into (it)' — می‌برآمیخت is an imperfect/progressive 'was mixing', در = 'into/in' (referring to some receptacle/context), and زهر = 'poison' as the object. Word order places the verb before the object phrase, typical of classical Persian narrative.

sentences.s760-s9: He swallowed it, and lived for another week after that; whoever heard (of it) wept.
sentences.s760-s10: Thus the kingship came to an end; from every side the enemy appeared.
sentences.s760-s11: Such is the conduct of the revolving age; watch and see what advantage you can obtain from it.
sentences.s760-s12: Eat whatever you have; do not rely on tomorrow, for tomorrow may bring another plan.
sentences.s760-s13: He takes from you and gives it to another; the world's banquet, without doubt, depends on effort.
sentences.s760-s14: Eat whatever you have; give away the surplus — do not be troubled on account of an enemy.
sentences.s760-s15: Whenever your day has gone by, all that you have laid down will become wind on the plain.
sentences.s760-s1: They summoned Farrokhzad from Jahrom and seated him upon that royal throne.
sentences.s760-s2: When he sat on the throne and cried 'Afarin', he praised the Creator for granting goodness to the world.
sentences.s760-s3: 'I am,' said the son of the shahanshah; 'I want nothing in the world except safety.'
sentences.s760-s4: Whoever in the world seeks to do harm — if I am king, he will not become exalted.
sentences.s760-s5: Whoever seeks truth in his heart will not bring deficiency into his inner affairs.
sentences.s760-s6: I will hold him as a precious, pure soul; I do not seek to do harm to the harmless.
sentences.s760-s7: When a month had passed on his throne, his head and his fortune came down to the earth.
sentences.s760-s8: Such was his state by day and in repose; meanwhile a certain servant was mixing poison.
